appeal_letter:
Dear Aetna Appeals Review,

I am writing to appeal the denial of outpatient speech therapy for [Member Name], dated [date]. The denial categorizes this patient's speech condition as developmental and therefore excluded under the adult medical benefit. This appeal asks you to review the clinical record, which documents a trauma-induced relapse, not a continuation of a childhood condition.

The patient did experience a stutter as a child, but fully resolved it. The treating provider documents that the stutter returned following a severe motor vehicle accident — a form of acquired neurogenic or trauma-related dysfluency that is clinically distinct from a developmental stutter. Aetna's own clinical policy covers restorative speech therapy for speech that was lost due to illness or injury. This patient had functioning speech before the accident and lost it as a result of the accident, placing this squarely within the restorative category.

The denial appears to have relied on the patient's history of childhood dysfluency without considering the intervening period of normal speech or the documented trauma that precipitated the current presentation. We ask that the reviewer consult the provider's notes documenting the pre-accident speech baseline and the onset of the current stutter in direct temporal relation to the accident.

We request a clinical review and reversal of this denial. Please confirm receipt and the deadline for any further submission.

This letter is a draft for your review.

feature_scores.evidence: Cites the pre-accident speech baseline and the trauma as the triggering event.
Correctly frames this as restorative (speech lost post-accident) rather than developmental.
Addresses the misclassification but would be stronger with a quoted provider note on pre-accident baseline.

gaps: moderate: case_specific_clinical_rebuttal (anchor 3) — a quoted provider note on the pre-accident baseline would strengthen this
outcome.critique: Good framing of the developmental-vs-restorative distinction; stronger with quoted clinical documentation.
rationale: The restorative framing directly addresses Aetna's policy language.
Temporal link between accident and stutter onset is the key evidentiary point.

risk_flags: Aetna may request independent clinical review; the provider's documentation of pre-accident speech baseline is critical to the outcome.

mirror.why_they_said_no: They say the stutter is a developmental condition from childhood, which is excluded under the adult medical benefit — only restorative therapy is covered.
mirror.deadline_note: Check your denial letter for the appeal filing deadline and submit before that date.
mirror.strongest_angle: The patient overcame the childhood stutter completely and had normal speech until the car accident — this is a trauma-induced relapse, which is restorative under Aetna's own policy language.

citations_used.title: Denial letter
citations_used.quote: clinical records indicate you have had a stutter since childhood, making this a developmental condition

citations_used.title: Aetna policy (referenced)
citations_used.quote: speech therapy when it is restorative in nature — meaning it is required to restore speech that was lost due to an illness, injury, or stroke

missing_evidence_checklist: Provider's note documenting normal pre-accident speech and onset of stutter following the accident
Accident report or medical records establishing temporal relationship
Speech-language pathologist's assessment documenting trauma-related dysfluency
